claims.claim_text: The module says a single Llama-70B inference uses 140GB of GPU memory, takes 30+ seconds, and that naive serving wastes 50-90% of GPU capacity.
claims.anchor_text: Large Language Models (LLMs) are expensive to serve. A single Llama-70B inference can use 140GB of GPU memory and take 30+ seconds. Naive serving approaches waste 50-90% of GPU capacity because of how attention memory is managed.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Large Language Models (LLMs) are expensive to serve, and inefficient KV-cache management can waste substantial GPU memory and limit concurrency.
claims.rationale: This combines several precise performance and memory figures that are not directly backed by the shared paper or current allowlisted docs.

claims.claim_text: The module claims vLLM achieves 2-24x higher throughput than Hugging Face TGI.
claims.anchor_text: Through innovations like PagedAttention and continuous batching, vLLM achieves 2-24x higher throughput than alternatives like Hugging Face's text-generation-inference.
claims.suggested_rewrite: The vLLM paper reports substantial throughput gains over prior serving systems, but the exact improvement depends on the workload, model, and baseline being compared.
claims.rationale: The shared paper supports 2-4x versus FasterTransformer and Orca, not 2-24x versus TGI.

claims.claim_text: The module says vLLM is the difference between serving 10 users or 200 users on the same hardware.
claims.anchor_text: It's the difference between serving 10 users or 200 users on the same hardware.
claims.suggested_rewrite: In practice, better serving efficiency can translate into much higher concurrency on the same hardware.
claims.rationale: This is an uncited illustrative concurrency claim with fake precision.

claims.claim_text: The quoted cost-savings testimonial is presented as a real outcome.
claims.anchor_text: > "We went from $50K/month in GPU costs to $8K/month. Same traffic, same model, just vLLM instead of vanilla transformers."
claims.suggested_rewrite: > Teams sometimes report large cost reductions after moving from less efficient serving stacks to vLLM, but the exact savings depend on workload and deployment details.
claims.rationale: This is a verbatim anecdotal quote with precise amounts and no attributable primary source.

claims.claim_text: PagedAttention was inspired by operating-system virtual memory techniques.
claims.rationale: The paper abstract explicitly says PagedAttention is inspired by classical virtual memory and paging techniques.

claims.claim_text: The module says vLLM can serve a 70B model on 2 GPUs instead of 8 with naive serving.
claims.anchor_text: - vLLM can serve a 70B parameter model on 2 GPUs that would normally require 8 GPUs with naive serving
claims.suggested_rewrite: - vLLM's memory management can reduce the GPU footprint needed to serve large models, but the exact GPU count depends on model size, precision, context length, and hardware.
claims.rationale: This exact GPU-count comparison is not directly supported by the shared paper or current allowlisted docs.

claims.claim_text: The project was created at UC Berkeley.
claims.rationale: The vLLM README says the project was originally developed in the Sky Computing Lab at UC Berkeley.

claims.claim_text: The module says vLLM achieved 24x throughput improvements in benchmarks.
claims.suggested_rewrite: published benchmark results showing substantial throughput gains over prior serving systems
claims.rationale: The allowlisted primary paper supports 2-4x in its reported evaluation, not 24x.

claims.claim_text: The module says vLLM supports 100+ model architectures including Llama, Mistral, Falcon, and GPT-NeoX.
claims.anchor_text: - vLLM supports **100+ model architectures** including Llama, Mistral, Falcon, GPT-NeoX, and more
claims.suggested_rewrite: - vLLM supports a large and growing set of model architectures, including Llama-family and Mistral-family models.
claims.rationale: The current allowlisted upstream README now states 200+ architectures, so this exact older count is not directly supported.

claims.claim_text: The module says continuous batching never waits for slow requests before starting new ones.
claims.anchor_text: - Continuous batching means vLLM **never waits** for slow requests to finish before starting new ones
claims.suggested_rewrite: - Continuous batching lets vLLM keep admitting work as capacity becomes available instead of relying on fixed static batches.
claims.rationale: The absolute 'never waits' phrasing overstates scheduler behavior relative to the cited upstream descriptions.

claims.claim_text: vLLM's memory efficiency comes from sharing KV cache pages between requests.
claims.rationale: The paper says vLLM enables flexible sharing of KV cache within and across requests to reduce memory usage.

claims.claim_text: The module requires a GPU with compute capability 7.0+.
claims.anchor_text: - NVIDIA GPU with compute capability 7.0+ (V100, A100, H100, etc.)
claims.suggested_rewrite: - A vLLM-supported NVIDIA GPU is required.
claims.rationale: I did not locate a current allowlisted primary doc in the approved domains that directly backs this exact prerequisite wording.

claims.claim_text: The module requires CUDA 11.8+.
claims.suggested_rewrite: - Install a CUDA and PyTorch stack supported by the vLLM release you use.
claims.rationale: Exact CUDA-minimum compatibility is version-sensitive and I did not find a stable allowlisted primary page for this precise floor.

claims.claim_text: The module says 16GB+ GPU memory is sufficient.
claims.suggested_rewrite: - GPU memory requirements vary significantly with model size, precision, and context length.
claims.rationale: This threshold is too specific to stand without a matching primary source and varies heavily by deployment.

claims.claim_text: The alternatives table says vLLM has the highest throughput, TGI is high, and Triton+TensorRT-LLM is very high.
claims.suggested_rewrite: | **Throughput** | High | High | High |
claims.rationale: These comparative superlatives are benchmark claims without a cited methodology or version-pinned source.

claims.claim_text: The alternatives table says vLLM has the best memory efficiency.
claims.suggested_rewrite: | **Memory Efficiency** | Strong KV-cache efficiency | Strong | Strong |
claims.rationale: This is an uncited comparative ranking rather than a source-backed factual statement.

claims.claim_text: The alternatives table gives exact model-support counts across systems.
claims.suggested_rewrite: | **Model Support** | Broad | Broad | More specialized |
claims.rationale: These counts are unstable and not directly backed by a single current primary page for all three systems.

claims.claim_text: The alternatives table lists exact quantization support across systems.
claims.anchor_text: | **Quantization** | AWQ, GPTQ, SqueezeLLM | GPTQ, AWQ | INT8, FP8 |
claims.suggested_rewrite: | **Quantization** | Multiple quantization options | Multiple quantization options | Multiple quantization options |
claims.rationale: These exact capability lists are version-sensitive and incomplete without version-pinned primary citations for each system.

claims.claim_text: The alternatives table compares multi-GPU support across systems.
claims.suggested_rewrite: | **Multi-GPU** | Supported | Supported | Supported |
claims.rationale: This row makes uncited cross-product capability judgments that depend on version and deployment mode.

claims.claim_text: The alternatives table says OpenAI API support is native in vLLM and TGI, and adapter-based in Triton+TensorRT-LLM.
claims.suggested_rewrite: | **OpenAI API** | OpenAI-compatible server available | API compatibility varies by deployment | Integration approach varies |
claims.rationale: This row makes exact compatibility claims across three products without current primary citations for each.

claims.claim_text: The alternatives table recommends each system for a particular use case.
claims.anchor_text: | **Best For** | Most use cases | HF ecosystem | Maximum perf |
claims.suggested_rewrite: | **Best For** | General-purpose high-throughput serving | Teams already invested in Hugging Face tooling | Highly optimized Triton/TensorRT deployments |
claims.rationale: These are uncited evaluative recommendations, not source-backed factual claims.

claims.claim_text: The war story says a startup was spending $180K/month on GPU costs.
claims.anchor_text: A startup building an AI writing assistant was spending $180K/month on GPU costs. They were using naive Hugging Face transformers inference:
claims.suggested_rewrite: An AI product team can spend heavily on GPU inference when its serving stack uses hardware inefficiently:
claims.rationale: This is an unattributed operational anecdote with precise financial figures and no primary source.

claims.claim_text: The war story specifies the model as Llama-2-13B.
claims.suggested_rewrite: - Model: a production LLM
claims.rationale: This is part of the same unattributed composite anecdote and should lose its fake-specific detail.

claims.claim_text: The war story says traffic was 50K requests/day.
claims.suggested_rewrite: - Traffic: enough sustained demand to make serving efficiency matter
claims.rationale: This is a precise operational metric with no cited source.

claims.claim_text: The war story says the infrastructure was 8x A100-40GB GPUs on AWS.
claims.suggested_rewrite: - Infrastructure: a multi-GPU cloud deployment
claims.rationale: This is an uncited infrastructure detail embedded in an unattributed anecdote.

claims.claim_text: The war story says GPU utilization was ~15%.
claims.anchor_text: - GPU utilization: ~15% (waiting on I/O most of the time)
claims.suggested_rewrite: - GPU utilization: low enough that expensive accelerators were underused
claims.rationale: This precise utilization figure is not attributable to a real cited incident or case study.

claims.claim_text: The war story says max concurrent users were 8.
claims.suggested_rewrite: - Concurrency: limited by the serving architecture
claims.rationale: This precise concurrency number is unsupported anecdotal detail.

claims.claim_text: The war story says average latency was 8 seconds.
claims.suggested_rewrite: - Latency: high enough to be a product problem
claims.rationale: This exact latency figure is part of an unattributed anecdote.

claims.claim_text: The war story says cost per inference was $0.04.
claims.suggested_rewrite: - Cost per inference: high enough to create budget pressure
claims.rationale: This exact cost figure is uncited and presented as a real operating metric.

claims.claim_text: The war story says GPU utilization rose to ~85% after migration.
claims.suggested_rewrite: - GPU utilization: materially higher after the serving redesign
claims.rationale: This exact post-migration utilization number is unsupported anecdotal detail.

claims.claim_text: The war story says max concurrent users became 256.
claims.suggested_rewrite: - Concurrency: substantially higher after the migration
claims.rationale: This precise concurrency claim is part of the same uncited anecdote.

claims.claim_text: The war story says average latency fell to 1.2 seconds.
claims.suggested_rewrite: - Latency: meaningfully lower after the migration
claims.rationale: This exact latency number is not sourced to a real incident or case study.

claims.claim_text: The war story says cost per inference fell to $0.006.
claims.suggested_rewrite: - Cost per inference: lower after improving serving efficiency
claims.rationale: This exact cost number is unsupported anecdotal detail.

claims.claim_text: The war story says monthly savings were $155K.
claims.suggested_rewrite: **Monthly savings: substantial, but workload-dependent**
claims.rationale: This exact savings figure is presented as a real result without a citable source.

further_reading.title: Efficient Memory Management for Large Language Model Serving with PagedAttention
further_reading.why_relevant: Primary research source for PagedAttention, KV-cache sharing, and the paper's reported throughput gains.

further_reading.title: vLLM OpenAI-Compatible Server
further_reading.why_relevant: Upstream documentation for vLLM's OpenAI-compatible APIs, including completions, chat, and embeddings endpoints.

further_reading.title: vLLM Parallelism and Scaling
further_reading.why_relevant: Upstream guidance on tensor parallelism, multi-GPU deployment, and `/dev/shm` considerations for serving.

further_reading.title: Schedule GPUs
further_reading.why_relevant: Authoritative Kubernetes documentation for requesting GPU resources in Pods and deployments.

notes: The local cold-start API briefing failed to start, but that did not block source review. Repeated versions of some unsupported claims also recur in the quiz and key takeaways, especially the 24x/2-24x throughput language and precise prefix-caching speedup; they should inherit the same cleanup if you want every occurrence normalized.
